cheque_bounce.legal_impact: Primary trigger for Section 138 NI Act

presumption_s139.legal_impact: Shifts burden of proof to the accused

vicarious_liability_issue.legal_impact: Mandatory requirement under Section 141 for corporate liability

partnership_and_firms.legal_impact: Requires impleadment of firm and all active partners under S.141

financial_capacity_risk.legal_impact: Complainant's financial capacity to lend large cash amounts can be challenged (Basalingappa rule)

cheque_bounce: Confirm receipt of Bank Return Memo stating reasons under S.138
Verify if the cheque was presented within 3 months of its date
K. Bhaskaran v. Sankaran Vaidhyan Balan (1999) 7 SCC 510
legal_notice_compliance: Check if demand notice was sent within 30 days of dishonour memo receipt
C.C. Alavi Haji v. Palapetty Muhammed (2007) 6 SCC 555
security_cheque: Evaluate if the debt was crystallized at the time of presentation
Apply S.139 presumption to shift burden to accused
Sampelly Satyanarayan Rao v. IREDA (2016) 10 SCC 458
Cheque issued as security creates significant defense rebuttable under S.139
signature_dispute: Disputed signature is a fatal evidentiary defect requiring forensic validation
signature_disputed: Disputed signature is a fatal evidentiary defect requiring forensic validation
no_debt_proof: Lack of documentary proof of debt weakens the S.139 presumption
notice_defect: Defective or unserved notice is a jurisdictional bar to prosecution
notice_not_sent: Failure to send statutory notice is a fatal procedural defect
limitation_issue: Time-barred complaints are non-maintainable under Section 142
cheque_misuse: Allegation of cheque misuse/theft requires strong rebuttal evidence
dishonour_disputed: Dispute over bank return reason weakens the primary evidence
payment_already_made: Proof of payment prior to notice expiry voids the cause of action
litigation_risk: Standard trial uncertainty and judicial discretion impact
financial_capacity_risk: High-value cash loans without source-of-fund proof are vulnerable to 'Financial Capacity' rebuttal

security_cheque: The cheque was issued as a security for a future transaction and not for any existing legally enforceable debt.
The S.139 presumption is rebuttable; if the accused shows that the debt was not crystallized on the presentation date, the case fails (Sampelly Satyanarayan Rao v. IREDA).
Sampelly Satyanarayan Rao v. IREDA (2016) 10 SCC 458
signature_dispute: The signature on the cheque is forged and does not belong to the accused.
Apply for forensic examination under S.45 Evidence Act. If forgery is proven, the entire S.138 action collapses as the instrument is void.
financial_capacity_risk: The complainant lacked the financial capacity to lend such a large sum of money in cash as claimed.
Shift the burden to the complainant to prove the source of funds. Lack of ITR or bank trail for large cash loans makes the transaction suspicious (Basalingappa v. Mudibasappa).
notice_defect: The statutory demand notice was not served within the mandatory 30-day window or contains material defects in the claim amount.
Section 138(b) compliance is a jurisdictional prerequisite. Any delay or defect in notice service is fatal to the prosecution.
C.C. Alavi Haji v. Palapetty Muhammed (2007) 6 SCC 555
